canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, TinyBenchmarks is consistently characterized as an evaluation suite designed for the efficient and rapid assessment of language models. It is defined as a collection of "small-scale datasets" or, more specifically, as "smaller, more efficient versions of popular benchmarks." The primary application described is measuring model performance on few-shot tasks, with one study citing its use for tasks such as "25-shot tinyARC, 5-shot GSM8K, 10-shot tinyHellaswag, 5-shot tinyWinoGrande." The recurring motivation for using this instrument is to achieve what one paper calls "efficient and low-cost evaluation." Another paper explicitly states it uses TinyBenchmarks "to further increase the efficiency of experiments," reinforcing its role in streamlined model assessment.
papers: Can We Predict Performance of Large Models across Vision-Language Tasks?
Speculate, then Collaborate: Fusing Knowledge of Language Models during Decoding
$\texttt{Model-GLUE}$: Democratized LLM Scaling for A Large Model Zoo in the Wild
Stealth edits to large language models

definitions.text: An evaluation suite of few-shot tasks designed to assess model performance with fewer examples.
definitions.paper: Stealth edits to large language models

definitions.text: A collection of small-scale datasets designed for efficient and low-cost evaluation of large language models.
definitions.paper: $\texttt{Model-GLUE}$: Democratized LLM Scaling for A Large Model Zoo in the Wild

definitions.text: tinyBenchmarks (Polo et al., 2024) is a suite of smaller, more efficient versions of popular benchmarks used for rapid evaluation of language models.
definitions.paper: Speculate, then Collaborate: Fusing Knowledge of Language Models during Decoding
